_meta.description: Demo script templates and judging criteria for Phase 3. Claude fills in the blanks instead of writing from scratch.

structure.prompt: Start with the problem. One sentence that makes judges nod.
examples: Ever tried to book a dog groomer and had to call 5 places?
Students waste 3 hours a week just figuring out what's due when.
Small coffee roasters lose 30% of potential sales because they have no online store.

structure.prompt: What you built and who it's for. Name the app.
structure.template: We built [APP NAME] — a [one-line description] for [target user]. It [main value prop].

structure.prompt: Show each page. Say what it does and why it matters. Point to specific numbers.
tips: Start with the dashboard — it's the most impressive
Point to specific data: 'You can see we have 156 customers and $4,280 in revenue this month'
Show 2-3 pages max — don't rush through everything
If you built a form, show it: 'Adding a new appointment takes 10 seconds'

structure.prompt: Brief mention of AI + team roles.
structure.template: We built this in [X] hours using AI as our coding partner. [CEO name] made the business decisions, [Designer name] guided the look and feel, and [Presenter name] — that's me — is showing it to you now.

structure.prompt: Where this goes next. One bold sentence.
examples: With real payments and notifications, this could launch in a month.
We'd add real-time scheduling and SMS reminders — that's a business I'd actually use.
This dashboard could help any small service business go from Post-it notes to professional in a day.

structure.prompt: Thank the judges. State team name.
structure.template: Thanks for watching. We're Team [NAME], and this is [APP NAME].

judgingCriteria.what: Does the app solve a real problem? Is there a clear customer?
Does the app look polished? Is the data realistic? Does it feel complete?
Did the team leverage AI effectively? Did they direct it well?
Was the demo clear, confident, and well-structured?
Is the idea unique or does it put a fresh spin on something?

presenterTips: Stand up when presenting — it projects confidence
Make eye contact with judges, not the screen
Point to specific numbers on screen ('We have 156 customers')
If something breaks during demo, laugh it off: 'Live demo magic!'
Practice the 2-minute version. If you go over, judges tune out
End strong — your last sentence is what judges remember
